title: Processes I — Kristen's Cookies
keyQuestion: How do we identify and analyze bottlenecks in business processes?
overview: Process analysis reveals the hidden constraints in every business operation. Using Kristen's Cookie Company, you'll learn to map workflows, identify bottlenecks (the slowest step that limits everything else), and calculate capacity. Think of it like finding the narrowest lane on a highway that causes all the traffic jams. The oven that bakes for 10 minutes becomes your constraint, not the mixing that takes 6 minutes. Setups (like cleaning between batches) and bottleneck shifting (when improvements move the constraint to a different step) add complexity that real managers must navigate every day.

coreContent.title: Process Flow Diagrams
coreContent.content:
### What Is a Process Flow Diagram?

A **process flow diagram** is a graphical representation of workflow in a business process. It shows every step, who performs it, and how long it takes.

#### Kristen's Cookies Process

| Step | Time | Resource |
|------|------|----------|
| Take Order | 1 min/order | You |
| Mix | 6 min/batch | You |
| Spoon | 2 min/doz | Roommate |
| Bake | 10 min/doz | Oven |
| Cool | 2 min/doz | Natural |
| Pack | 1 min/order | You |
| Pay | 5 min/order | You |

#### Key Time Definitions

- **Throughput Time** (also called **Flow Time** or **Turn-Around Time**): The time required to complete a given job — from the time it enters the system until it leaves
- **Lead Time**: The time between the *placement* and *fulfillment* of the order (broader — includes time before production starts)

> 💡 Drawing the process flow diagram is the **first step** in any process analysis — it reveals the sequence of activities and helps identify potential bottlenecks.
coreContent.keyInsight: Drawing the process flow diagram is the first step in any process analysis - it reveals the sequence of activities and helps identify potential bottlenecks.

buildsInto: C20 (Little's Law) — throughput time is a key variable in WIP = Throughput Rate × Throughput Time
C30 (Queueing) — process flow reveals where queues form
examAppearances: CC1 Q1: Draw process flow and calculate throughput time for Kristen's Cookies
Midterm 2026 Q1: Station-by-station process analysis for Factory Physics problem

coreContent.title: Cycle Time and Capacity
coreContent.content:
### Cycle Time

**Cycle Time** ($\text{time/unit}$, e.g., $\text{min/dozen}$): The time between successive completions of a batch.

> The cycle time for the **system** is the same as the cycle time of the **slowest stage** (the bottleneck).

### Capacity (Throughput Rate)

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{1}{\text{Cycle Time}}$$

**Throughput Rate** ($\text{units/time}$, e.g., $\text{dozen/hr}$) is the rate at which work gets done. The **maximum possible** throughput rate is the **capacity** of the system.

#### Important Distinctions
- **Cycle time** = time *between* successive outputs (the gap)
- **Throughput time** = total time for *one unit* to traverse the entire system (the journey)
- Capacity can be defined for an **individual stage** or the **entire system**
coreContent.keyInsight: System capacity equals the capacity of the bottleneck resource - not the sum of all resources.

buildsInto: C03 (Flow Rate) — flow rate depends on capacity vs. demand
C09 (Capacity Analysis) — systematic method to find capacity of each resource
examAppearances: CC1 Q2: Calculate cycle time and capacity for multi-station process
Midterm 2026 Q1: Capacity calculations with setup times and batch sizes

coreContent.title: Bottleneck Analysis
coreContent.content:
### What Is a Bottleneck?

A **bottleneck** is the resource that limits the processing activity. It is the stage with the **longest cycle time** (or **least capacity**).

#### Kristen's Cookies — Finding the Bottleneck (1-dozen orders)

| Resource | Time per Dozen | Capacity |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| **You** | 8 min/doz | $60/8 = 7.5$ doz/hr |
| **Oven** | 10 min/doz | $60/10 = 6$ doz/hr |
| **Roommate** | 4 min/doz | $60/4 = 15$ doz/hr |

**Bottleneck = Oven** at $6$ doz/hr (lowest capacity)

### ⚠️ Bottleneck Shifting

**Critical concept:** Doubling the capacity of the bottleneck will **NOT** necessarily double system capacity — because the **bottleneck may shift** to another resource!

- Bottlenecks may move as **order size changes**
- There may be **more than one bottleneck** simultaneously
- After fixing the bottleneck, **always re-check** which step is now the constraint
coreContent.keyInsight: Bottlenecks may move as order size changes. Doubling capacity of bottleneck resource will not necessarily double system capacity because the bottleneck may shift.

buildsInto: C13 (Bottleneck Shifting) — what happens when you fix one bottleneck
C09 (Capacity Analysis) — systematic method for finding bottlenecks
C23 (WIP-Throughput Tradeoff) — bottleneck determines the throughput ceiling in Factory Physics curves
examAppearances: CC1 Q2: Identify bottleneck and calculate flow rate for multi-station process
Midterm 2026 Q1: Bottleneck shifts from Station D to Station A when batch size changes
Midterm 2026 Q1: Optimal batch size to maximize capacity (~12-13 units)

coreContent.title: Setup Effects on Capacity
coreContent.content:
### What Is Setup Time?

**Setup time** is the time needed to prepare a machine, person, or system for a new task — from the end of the last batch to the start of the first good piece of the next batch.

#### Types of Setups
- Cleaning
- Tool changes
- Adjustments
- Programming
- Test runs

### Three Categories of Setup Impact

| Category | Capacity vs. Batch Size | Example |
|----------|------------------------|----------|
| **No setups** | Capacity does **not** depend on order size | Continuous flow process |
| **Uncapacitated setups** | Capacity **increases** (at decreasing rate) as batch size increases | Machine with changeover time |
| **Capacitated setups** | Capacity may **increase or decrease** with batch size, depending on the "mixing bowl" constraint | Kristen's mixer (limited bowl size) |

### Capacity with Setups Formula

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{\text{Batch Size}}{\text{Setup Time} + (\text{Processing Time per Unit} \times \text{Batch Size})}$$

> 💡 **Setups create economies of scale** — larger batches spread the setup time across more units, improving per-unit capacity.
coreContent.keyInsight: Setups create economies of scale - larger batches spread the setup time across more units, improving capacity.

buildsInto: C50 (EOQ) — the fixed ordering cost in EOQ is essentially a setup cost
C01 (Bottleneck) — setups can change which resource is the bottleneck
examAppearances: Midterm 2026 Q1: Setup times change bottleneck when batch size shrinks (Station A becomes bottleneck)
Midterm 2026 Q1: Optimal batch size balances setup frequency vs. capacity (~12-13 units)
CC1 Q3: Capacity calculations with setup time included

coreContent.title: Flow Rate and Utilization
coreContent.content:
### Flow Rate

$$\text{Flow Rate} = \min(\text{Demand Rate}, \text{Process Capacity})$$

| Scenario | Condition | Implication |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Capacity-constrained** | Demand > Capacity | Flow rate = Capacity; you're losing potential sales |
| **Demand-constrained** | Capacity > Demand | Flow rate = Demand; you have idle resources |

### Utilization

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{\text{Flow Rate}}{\text{Capacity}} = \frac{\text{Time Busy}}{\text{Time Available}}$$

#### Kristen's Cookies Example
With oven as bottleneck at $6$ doz/hr:

$$\text{Utilization of You (mixing)} = \frac{6 \text{ doz/hr}}{7.5 \text{ doz/hr}} = 80\%$$

> 💡 **Non-bottleneck resources will always have utilization less than 100%.** Only the bottleneck determines the flow rate of the entire system.
coreContent.keyInsight: Non-bottleneck resources will have utilization less than 100%. Only the bottleneck determines the flow rate of the entire system.

prerequisiteConcepts: C01 (Bottleneck) — must identify bottleneck to determine process capacity
C02 (Process Capacity) — capacity is the denominator in utilization
buildsInto: C04 → C30 (Queueing) — utilization ($\rho$) drives the hockey-stick waiting time curve
C04 → C32 (Utilization Effect) — $\rho/(1-\rho)$ shows why high utilization = long waits
examAppearances: CC1 Q2: Calculate utilization for each station in multi-station process
Midterm 2026 Q1: Determine whether system is demand-constrained or capacity-constrained
Midterm 2026 Q1: Calculate station utilizations under different batch sizes

coreContent.title: Capacity Analysis Tool
coreContent.content:
### The Systematic Approach

**Tool: Capacity Analysis** — Determine capacity of each resource in isolation, then find the constraint.

#### Steps:
1. **Calculate processing time per unit** for each resource
2. **Convert to capacity** (units per hour): $\text{Capacity} = 60 / \text{processing time (min)}$
3. **Identify bottleneck** — the resource with the **lowest capacity**
4. **Calculate flow rate**: $\text{Flow Rate} = \min(\text{Demand}, \text{Bottleneck Capacity})$
5. **Calculate utilizations** for each resource: $\text{Utilization} = \text{Flow Rate} / \text{Station Capacity}$
6. **Analyze impact of changes** — adding resources, changing batch sizes, reducing setups

### Watch for These Traps
- Setup times that **change with batch size**
- **Shared resources** that serve multiple steps
- **Workers who do different tasks** (aggregate their time per unit)
- Batch size effects on bottleneck identity

> 💡 Capacity analysis is the **foundation** for all process improvement decisions — it shows where to focus your efforts for maximum impact.
coreContent.keyInsight: Capacity analysis is the foundation for all process improvement decisions - it shows where to focus your efforts for maximum impact.

prerequisiteConcepts: C01 (Bottleneck) — need to understand bottleneck concept
C02 (Process Capacity) — calculating capacity for each resource
C06 (Cycle Time) — converting between time and rate
buildsInto: C13 (Bottleneck Shifting) — what happens after you improve the bottleneck
C20 (Little's Law) — connects capacity analysis to WIP and throughput time
examAppearances: CC1 Q2: Full capacity analysis for Power Toys assembly line
Midterm 2026 Q1: Capacity analysis with setup times for 4-station process
Final Review: Capacity analysis appears as foundation for queueing problems

formulas.name: Capacity Formula
formulas.workedExample: Oven cycle time = 10 min/dozen = 0.167 hr/dozen. Capacity = 1 ÷ 0.167 = 6 dozen/hour

formulas.name: Flow Rate Formula
formulas.formula: Flow Rate = min(Demand Rate, Process Capacity)
formulas.workedExample: If demand = 8 dozen/hr and process capacity = 6 dozen/hr, then flow rate = 6 dozen/hr

formulas.name: Utilization Formula

variables.Utilization: Percentage of time resource is busy

formulas.workedExample: Mixing capacity = 7.5 dozen/hr, Flow rate = 6 dozen/hr, Utilization = 6/7.5 = 80%

formulas.name: Capacity with Setups
formulas.formula: Capacity = Batch Size / (Setup Time + Processing Time per Unit × Batch Size)
formulas.workedExample: Setup = 110 min, processing = 8 min/unit, batch = 40: Capacity = 40/(110 + 8×40) = 40/430 = 0.093 batches/min = 5.58 units/hr

practiceProblems.question:
### Kristen's Cookie Throughput Time

For Kristen's Cookie Company with **one oven**, calculate the **throughput time** for an order of one dozen cookies. Show the Gantt chart timing for each step.

**Process steps:**
| Step | Time | Resource |
|------|------|----------|
| Take Order | $1$ min | You |
| Mix | $6$ min | You |
| Spoon | $2$ min | Roommate |
| Bake | $10$ min | Oven |
| Cool | $2$ min | Natural |
| Pack | $1$ min | You |
| Pay | $5$ min | You |
practiceProblems.solution:
#### Gantt Chart Timing

| Step | Start | End | Resource |
|------|-------|-----|----------|
| Take Order | $0$ | $1$ min | You |
| Mix | $1$ | $7$ min | You |
| Spoon | $7$ | $9$ min | Roommate |
| Bake | $9$ | $19$ min | Oven |
| Cool | $19$ | $21$ min | Natural |
| Pack | $21$ | $22$ min | You |
| Pay | $22$ | $27$ min | You |

$$\text{Total throughput time} = 27 \text{ minutes for one dozen cookies}$$

> 💡 Note: For subsequent orders, the throughput time per order decreases because steps can **overlap** (pipelining). The oven bakes order 1 while You mix order 2.
practiceProblems.source: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts Haas 2026.json

practiceProblems.question:
### Power Toys Assembly Line

Power Toys Inc. produces toy trucks on a **9-station assembly line**.

| Station | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time (sec) | $72$ | $80$ | $90$ | $68$ | $70$ | $60$ | $95$ | $58$ | $80$ |

**Calculate:**
1. Which station is the bottleneck?
2. What is the system capacity (trucks/hour)?
3. If demand is $60$ trucks/hour, what is the flow rate?
4. What is the utilization of Station 2?
practiceProblems.solution:
#### Step 1: Identify Bottleneck
**Station 7** has the longest processing time at $95$ seconds → **Bottleneck**

#### Step 2: System Capacity
$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{3600 \text{ sec/hr}}{95 \text{ sec/unit}} = 37.9 \text{ trucks/hr}$$

#### Step 3: Flow Rate
$$\text{Flow Rate} = \min(60, 37.9) = 37.9 \text{ trucks/hr}$$

The system is **capacity-constrained** (demand exceeds capacity).

#### Step 4: Utilization of Station 2
$$\text{Station 2 Capacity} = \frac{3600}{80} = 45 \text{ trucks/hr}$$
$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{37.9}{45} = 84.2\%$$

> 💡 Station 2 is **not** the bottleneck, so its utilization is below $100\%$.
practiceProblems.source: Capacity Analysis After Class Practice Problems and Solutions.json

practiceProblems.question:
### Setup Time and Batch Size

A machine has:
- **Setup time:** $110$ minutes
- **Processing time per unit:** $8$ minutes

Compare the capacity for batch sizes of **40 units** vs. **100 units**.
practiceProblems.solution:
#### Batch Size = 40 Units
$$\text{Total time} = 110 + (8 \times 40) = 110 + 320 = 430 \text{ minutes}$$
$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{40 \text{ units}}{430 \text{ min}} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 5.58 \text{ units/hr}$$

#### Batch Size = 100 Units
$$\text{Total time} = 110 + (8 \times 100) = 110 + 800 = 910 \text{ minutes}$$
$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{100 \text{ units}}{910 \text{ min}} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 6.59 \text{ units/hr}$$

#### Comparison

| Batch Size | Total Time | Capacity | Setup % of Total |
|-----------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| $40$ | $430$ min | $5.58$ units/hr | $110/430 = 25.6\%$ |
| $100$ | $910$ min | $6.59$ units/hr | $110/910 = 12.1\%$ |

> 💡 Larger batches **spread setup cost** across more units, increasing effective capacity. But the improvement has **diminishing returns** — going from $40$ to $100$ (+150%) only increases capacity by $18\%$.
practiceProblems.source: Capacity Analysis After Class Practice Problems and Solutions.json

keyInsights: Three things that hurt you in operations: Setups, Bottlenecks, Uncertainty & Variability
System performance is determined by the limiting resource (bottleneck capacity)
Capacity may depend on order size (or batch size) due to setups
Doubling capacity of bottleneck resource will not necessarily double system capacity
Bottlenecks may move as order size changes
There may be more than one bottleneck
Tool: Capacity Analysis - Determine capacity of each resource in isolation
Tool: Setup Reduction - Find ways to speed up setup times, set up offline, specialized setup personnel
